refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure and report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint on the system's operation, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The refined FR focuses on the functional capability—providing visibility into energy efficiency—which is solution-neutral and measurable. Achievement of the energy_per_ton ≤ E_target acceptance criterion is a system-level performance requirement that emerges from the combined design of the NGHE solution (DP and IM layers), not a single FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution provides a mechanism (e.g., telemetry interface, dashboard, data export) to capture energy_used and ton_moved over the evaluation period, and that reported values are traceable to instrumentation with documented accuracy. Confirm that Site Manager and System Engineer can retrieve and validate energy efficiency metrics without manual calculation.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure total energy consumed during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure total work output (mass moved or equivalent productivity metric) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute and report the ratio of energy consumed to work output (energy_per_ton or equivalent).
FR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall provide access to energy efficiency metrics to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, System Engineer) for evaluation and analysis.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio offline post-evaluation period

DP2: Embed real-time energy and tonnage sensors with local data aggregation; stream normalized metrics to central analytics engine; compute and report energy_per_ton continuously with periodic validation

DP3: Decompose energy consumption into sub-processes (transport, loading, unloading); assign work output proportionally to each; compute energy efficiency per process stage; aggregate to system-level energy_per_ton
DP4: Establish energy baseline from historical operational data; measure incremental energy consumption and tonnage during evaluation period; compute delta energy_per_ton relative to baseline

DP5: Implement dual-path measurement: primary path uses calibrated energy and tonnage instruments with redundant sensors; secondary path uses independent estimation model (e.g., fuel consumption proxy, load cell cross-check); reconcile both paths to validate energy_per_ton
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP2: Embed real-time energy and tonnage sensors with local data aggregation; stream normalized metrics to central analytics engine; compute and report energy_per_ton continuously with periodic validation

potential_sources: Sensor placement coupling: energy measurement location (fuel tank, battery, grid connection) must align with system boundary definition; tonnage measurement location (scale at destination, load cell on vehicle) must be unambiguous—risk of double-counting or omission if boundaries not clearly owned

Temporal coupling: real-time streaming introduces dependency on network availability and clock synchronization; if either fails, energy_per_ton computation is delayed or invalid—mitigate via local buffering and graceful degradation

Calibration coupling: energy and tonnage sensors must be calibrated to same reference standard and evaluation period; drift in either sensor invalidates ratio—mitigate via periodic cross-validation against DP5 secondary path

Operational policy coupling: definition of 'work output' (e.g., does tonnage include return journey? partial loads?) must be locked in worksite safety plan before measurement begins—ambiguity couples FR to operational context
coupling_check.notes: DP2 is preferred over DP1 (batch offline) because continuous measurement enables early detection of efficiency degradation and supports adaptive operational decisions. DP2 avoids DP3's process-level decomposition complexity (which introduces allocation ambiguity) and DP4's baseline dependency (which obscures absolute efficiency). DP5 is complementary validation, not primary DP. Coupling is manageable if sensor ownership, calibration schedule, and work-output definition are established as explicit architectural contracts before implementation.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Energy source is metered at a single, well-defined point (e.g., fuel pump, battery charger, grid connection). RISK: If energy is sourced from multiple points (e.g., on-site generator + grid), measurement boundary becomes ambiguous. MITIGATION: Clarify energy system architecture in design phase; if multi-source, establish aggregation rule in DP.

ASSUMPTION: Tonnage measurement is performed at a single, authoritative point (e.g., certified scale at destination). RISK: If tonnage is estimated from load cell readings or driver reports, measurement error propagates directly into energy_per_ton ratio. MITIGATION: Specify measurement instrument class (e.g., NIST-traceable) and validation frequency in acceptance criteria.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is continuous and uninterrupted. RISK: If operations are seasonal or intermittent, energy_per_ton may be computed over periods with low tonnage, inflating ratio. MITIGATION: Define evaluation period explicitly (e.g., 90 consecutive operational days, minimum 500 tons moved) in acceptance criteria.

ASSUMPTION: Energy consumption is primarily driven by work output (tonnage moved). RISK: If energy is consumed by idle equipment, climate control, or administrative systems, energy_per_ton will be artificially high and not reflect transport efficiency. MITIGATION: Establish energy boundary (e.g., 'traction energy only' vs. 'total site energy') in FR refinement; if boundary includes non-work energy, adjust E_target accordingly.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are stable during evaluation period. RISK: If policies change (e.g., speed limits, route changes, load restrictions), energy_per_ton may shift due to operational factors, not equipment efficiency. MITIGATION: Freeze operational policies during evaluation period; document any changes and their impact on energy_per_ton.

ASSUMPTION: Sensor data is trustworthy and not subject to tampering or calibration drift. RISK: If sensors are not regularly validated, reported energy_per_ton may be systematically biased. MITIGATION: Implement DP5 secondary validation path; establish sensor recalibration schedule (e.g., quarterly).

ASSUMPTION: Central analytics engine is available and reliable. RISK: If analytics engine fails, real-time reporting is interrupted and energy_per_ton cannot be computed. MITIGATION: Implement local data buffering and offline computation fallback; define SLA for analytics availability.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Energy boundary: 'Energy consumption shall be measured at [fuel pump | battery charger | grid connection] and include [traction energy only | all site energy].' (2) Work output definition: 'Tonnage moved shall be measured at [destination scale | vehicle load cell] and include [loaded trips only | loaded + empty return trips].' (3) Evaluation period: 'Energy_per_ton shall be computed over [X consecutive operational days | minimum Y tons moved], with operational policies frozen per attached worksite safety plan.' (4) Acceptance validation: 'Measured energy_per_ton shall satisfy ≤ E_target with ±Z% measurement uncertainty (95% confidence), validated via [secondary measurement path | independent audit].' These refinements eliminate ambiguity in DP2 implementation and reduce coupling risk.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized real-time energy and tonnage analytics platform encompassing sensor integration, local aggregation logic, streaming pipeline, central computation engine, and continuous KPI reporting (energy_per_ton)

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives raw energy and tonnage sensor streams from Vehicle; normalized metrics flow inbound to central analytics engine

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outputs computed energy_per_ton KPIs and periodic validation reports to operational tasking and fleet management systems

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication for sensor data ingestion and metric distribution; supports streaming and batch validation cycles

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest cohesion: DP2 is fundamentally an analytics and KPI computation platform. Sensor embedding and local aggregation are Vehicle responsibilities (external), but central streaming, normalization, computation, and reporting are core NGHE functions. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry inbound and Tasking/Network outbound required.

Poor fit: Vehicle owns physical sensors and local aggregation hardware, but central analytics engine, streaming pipeline, and continuous KPI computation belong in backend infrastructure. Significant diffusion: energy_per_ton logic would leak into vehicle firmware, creating tight coupling to analytics algorithms and requiring vehicle updates for metric changes.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation is operator-facing control and visualization. Real-time sensor aggregation and analytics computation are orthogonal to remote operation. High diffusion: would require embedding analytics logic in operator interface, violating separation of concerns.

Poor fit: Safety Services handles hazard detection and alerts. Energy efficiency metrics are operational KPIs, not safety-critical. Containment here would conflate safety logic with performance analytics. High diffusion: energy_per_ton computation would diffuse across safety validation and alert generation, creating unnecessary coupling.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP2 is best contained within NGHE Solution because: (1) the DP explicitly specifies 'central analytics engine' and 'continuous reporting'—both backend platform responsibilities; (2) sensor embedding and local aggregation are Vehicle concerns (external input via Telemetry), but normalization, streaming, computation, and KPI generation are NGHE core functions; (3) Vehicle remains focused on hardware and local data collection; (4) Teleoperation and Safety Services remain decoupled from analytics logic; (5) all external dependencies (Telemetry inbound, Tasking/Network outbound) are well-defined and minimal.
containment_risks: Risk: Vehicle-side local aggregation logic may require iterative refinement based on central analytics feedback, creating tight coupling if not properly versioned.
Risk: Streaming pipeline reliability and latency SLAs must be contractually defined between Vehicle (data source) and NGHE Solution (consumer) to prevent diffusion of performance concerns.
Risk: Periodic validation logic may require access to historical sensor data or safety context, potentially requiring additional interfaces to Safety Services or external data stores.
Risk: If energy_per_ton thresholds trigger operational decisions (e.g., task reassignment), Tasking interface may become bidirectional, increasing coupling complexity.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP2 directly addresses FR requirement: 'embed real-time energy and tonnage sensors with local data aggregation; stream normalized metrics to central analytics engine; compute and report energy_per_ton continuously' maps precisely to 'measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output'. The DP specifies the complete logical chain (sensor → aggregation → streaming → computation → reporting) required to satisfy the FR. Acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is measurable and traceable to DP2's continuous KPI reporting capability.

diffusion_risks: Vehicle-side local aggregation logic coupling: Iterative refinement of aggregation algorithms based on central analytics feedback could create tight coupling if versioning and interface contracts are not enforced. Recommend explicit API versioning and deprecation policy between Vehicle (data source) and NGHE Solution (consumer).

Streaming pipeline SLA diffusion: Reliability and latency requirements for the streaming pipeline must be contractually defined and monitored. Absence of explicit SLAs risks diffusion of performance concerns across Vehicle and NGHE Solution ownership boundaries.

Periodic validation logic scope creep: Validation may require access to historical sensor data, safety context, or external data stores. If validation logic expands to include safety-related checks, it risks diffusion into Safety Services domain. Recommend explicit boundary definition: validation is performance-only, not safety-critical.

Tasking interface bidirectionality: If computed energy_per_ton thresholds trigger operational decisions (task reassignment, vehicle reallocation), the Tasking interface may become bidirectional, increasing coupling complexity and decision-making diffusion. Recommend unidirectional reporting with explicit decision logic owned by Tasking/Fleet Management, not NGHE Solution.

Sensor embedding responsibility ambiguity: DP2 states 'embed real-time energy and tonnage sensors' but IM boundary assigns sensor hardware to Vehicle (external). Clarify: NGHE Solution owns sensor *specification and validation logic*; Vehicle owns physical *installation and local hardware*. This prevents diffusion of sensor calibration logic across boundaries.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score with minimal diffusion (0.08). The DP is well-aligned with IM boundary: central analytics engine, streaming pipeline, normalization, computation, and KPI reporting are core NGHE functions. Vehicle remains focused on hardware and local aggregation (external input via Telemetry interface). Teleoperation and Safety Services are appropriately decoupled. Three external interfaces (Telemetry inbound, Tasking outbound, Network bidirectional) are minimal and well-defined. Recommend formalizing interface contracts (data schemas, SLAs, versioning policies) to prevent future diffusion as system evolves.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Acceptance criterion 'energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over agreed evaluation period' is directly verifiable through NGHE Solution's continuous KPI reporting capability. IM boundary includes 'continuous KPI reporting' and 'periodic validation', enabling measurement against target. Worksite safety plan and operational policies are external constraints (not owned by NGHE Solution) but do not impede verification of energy efficiency metric. Recommend: (1) define E_target value and evaluation period explicitly in requirements; (2) establish data retention and audit trail requirements for validation reports; (3) specify reporting frequency and format for stakeholder consumption.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns end-to-end analytics and reporting; Vehicle owns sensor hardware and local aggregation (external); Tasking/Fleet Management owns operational decision-making based on reported KPIs. However, recommend explicit allocation of three responsibilities: (1) Sensor specification and validation logic ownership (NGHE Solution or Vehicle?); (2) Streaming pipeline SLA ownership and monitoring (NGHE Solution or Network/Infrastructure team?); (3) Periodic validation logic scope and data access rights (NGHE Solution only, or shared with Safety Services for context?). Clarifying these will eliminate residual ambiguity and prevent future diffusion.

recommended_revisions: Revise DP2 to explicitly separate concerns: 'Embed real-time energy and tonnage sensors [Vehicle responsibility] with local data aggregation [Vehicle responsibility]; stream normalized metrics to central analytics engine [NGHE Solution responsibility]; compute and report energy_per_ton continuously with periodic validation [NGHE Solution responsibility].' This clarifies the FR->DP->IM chain and prevents diffusion.

Add explicit interface contracts: (1) Telemetry interface—define sensor data schema, sampling rate, latency SLA, and versioning policy; (2) Tasking interface—define KPI report schema, reporting frequency, and decision-making ownership (NGHE reports, Tasking decides); (3) Network interface—define streaming protocol, batch validation cycle timing, and reliability requirements.

Define sensor specification and validation ownership: Recommend NGHE Solution owns sensor *specification, calibration algorithms, and validation logic*; Vehicle owns *physical installation and local hardware maintenance*. This prevents diffusion of calibration logic into vehicle firmware.

Establish streaming pipeline SLA and ownership: Recommend explicit SLA for streaming latency, throughput, and reliability. Assign ownership to NGHE Solution (computation) and Network/Infrastructure team (transport), with clear escalation paths. This prevents diffusion of performance concerns.

Scope periodic validation logic explicitly: Recommend validation is *performance-only* (energy_per_ton accuracy, sensor drift detection, data quality checks). Exclude safety-related validation to prevent diffusion into Safety Services. If safety context is needed, define read-only access contract with Safety Services.

Clarify Tasking interface directionality: Recommend unidirectional reporting (NGHE Solution → Tasking). If energy_per_ton thresholds trigger operational decisions, assign decision logic ownership to Tasking/Fleet Management, not NGHE Solution. This prevents diffusion of operational decision-making.

Add acceptance criteria detail: Specify E_target value, evaluation period duration, reporting frequency, and validation cycle timing. Recommend: 'energy_per_ton ≤ E_target [value] measured over [period] with [frequency] reporting and [cycle] validation.' This enables unambiguous verification.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence (0.92 containment, minimal diffusion 0.08). However, the recommended_revisions identify seven critical clarifications required before decomposition can proceed safely. The primary issue is allocation ambiguity across three responsibility boundaries: (1) sensor specification/validation ownership, (2) streaming pipeline SLA ownership, (3) periodic validation scope relative to Safety Services. Given high safety criticality, these ambiguities must be resolved at the current level before deriving sub-FRs. Proceeding to decomposition without clarification risks diffusion of safety-critical logic into NGHE Solution or unintended coupling with Safety Services. Revise DP2 and interface contracts first, then re-gate before sub-FR derivation.

revise.why: DP2 requires explicit separation of concerns across ownership boundaries. Current DP conflates Vehicle responsibilities (sensor embedding, local aggregation) with NGHE Solution responsibilities (streaming, normalization, computation, reporting). Revise to: 'Vehicle embeds real-time energy and tonnage sensors with local data aggregation and streams normalized metrics via Telemetry interface. NGHE Solution receives streamed metrics, aggregates across fleet, computes energy_per_ton continuously, performs periodic validation (performance-only, excluding safety context), and reports KPIs to Tasking/Fleet Management via unidirectional Tasking interface.' This clarifies FR->DP->IM chain and prevents diffusion. Additionally, add three explicit interface contract specifications: (1) Telemetry—sensor data schema, sampling rate, latency SLA, versioning policy; (2) Tasking—KPI report schema, reporting frequency, decision ownership (NGHE reports, Tasking decides); (3) Network—streaming protocol, batch validation cycle timing, reliability SLA. Assign ownership: NGHE Solution owns sensor specification/calibration algorithms/validation logic; Vehicle owns physical installation/hardware maintenance; Network/Infrastructure owns streaming SLA; Tasking/Fleet Management owns operational decision-making.

assumptions: E_target value and evaluation period are defined externally (not in scope of current FR decomposition).
Vehicle platform (external) is capable of embedding sensors and performing local aggregation without architectural changes to NGHE Solution.
Tasking/Fleet Management system (external) can consume unidirectional KPI reports and owns operational decision-making logic.
Safety Services domain is separate from NGHE Solution and does not require real-time energy metrics for safety-critical decisions.
Network infrastructure (external) can support streaming pipeline with defined SLA for latency, throughput, and reliability.
Periodic validation logic is performance-only (sensor drift, data quality, energy_per_ton accuracy) and does not include safety-related checks.
open_risks: Sensor specification and validation logic ownership ambiguity: If NGHE Solution owns sensor calibration algorithms but Vehicle owns hardware installation, version mismatches between algorithm updates and firmware could cause data quality issues. Recommend explicit versioning and deprecation policy.

Streaming pipeline SLA diffusion: If streaming latency or reliability degrades, responsibility for remediation is unclear (NGHE Solution vs. Network/Infrastructure). Recommend explicit SLA contract with escalation paths.

Periodic validation scope creep: If validation logic expands to include safety-related checks (e.g., anomaly detection for equipment failure), it risks diffusion into Safety Services domain. Recommend explicit boundary: validation is performance-only.

Tasking interface bidirectionality risk: If energy_per_ton thresholds trigger automatic operational decisions (task reassignment, vehicle reallocation), decision-making logic may diffuse into NGHE Solution. Recommend unidirectional reporting with decision logic owned by Tasking/Fleet Management.

Data retention and audit trail requirements: Periodic validation reports must be retained for compliance and forensic analysis. Ownership of data retention policy and audit trail is unclear. Recommend explicit allocation.

Safety criticality interaction: High safety criticality of the overall system (worksite operations) may require NGHE Solution to provide safety-relevant context (e.g., equipment stress indicators) to Safety Services. Current DP does not address this interaction. Recommend explicit boundary definition with Safety Services.
evidence_needed: Explicit definition of E_target value (energy_per_ton threshold) and evaluation period (e.g., per shift, per week, per project).
Sensor specification document: sensor types, accuracy requirements, sampling rates, calibration procedures, and ownership of calibration logic.
Streaming pipeline SLA: latency, throughput, reliability targets, and ownership of monitoring and remediation.

Interface contract specifications: (1) Telemetry—sensor data schema (JSON/Protobuf), sampling rate (Hz), latency SLA (ms), versioning policy; (2) Tasking—KPI report schema, reporting frequency (real-time/batch), decision ownership clarification; (3) Network—streaming protocol (Kafka/MQTT/gRPC), batch validation cycle timing, reliability SLA (99.x%).

Periodic validation logic specification: scope (sensor drift detection, data quality checks, energy_per_ton accuracy validation), data access rights, exclusion of safety-related checks, and reporting format.

Safety Services interaction analysis: clarify whether NGHE Solution must provide safety-relevant context (equipment stress, anomalies) to Safety Services, and if so, define read-only data access contract.

Acceptance criteria detail: specify E_target value, evaluation period duration, reporting frequency, validation cycle timing, and data retention requirements. Example: 'energy_per_ton ≤ 2.5 kWh/ton measured over 1-week evaluation period with daily KPI reporting and weekly validation cycles; validation reports retained for 12 months.'
